title: Use RECORD files for shared cache populate
description: populate_shared_cache uses heuristic name matching to find wheel files. Instead, read dist-info/RECORD which lists every file the wheel installed. Handles namespace packages, custom import names, etc.
close_reason: Closed

title: Atomic shared cache population
description: Two processes installing the same wheel can race on populate_shared_cache. Use staging dir + rename pattern (like extract does) to make cache writes atomic.
close_reason: Closed

title: Cache pylock.toml resolution output by env_key
description: uv pip compile runs every cold start even for identical requirements. Cache the pylock.toml output keyed by env_key so repeat cold starts skip resolution (2-5s saving). Store at $ZEROSTART_CACHE/pylock/{env_key}.toml
close_reason: Closed

title: Populate shared cache from warm command
description: zerostart warm extracts wheels but doesn't call populate_shared_cache. Add it so multi-environment workflows benefit from the shared CUDA cache.
close_reason: Closed

title: Transparent Model Loading Acceleration
description:
Eliminate GPU model loading bottleneck (35-40s) with transparent hooks that require zero user code changes. One line — `zerostart.accelerate()` — or CLI flag `--accelerate` patches from_pretrained, safetensors, and torch.load for 4x faster model loading.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] `zerostart.accelerate()` transparently speeds up transformers from_pretrained
- [ ] Safetensors network volume fix eliminates 30-50x mmap penalty on FUSE/NFS
- [ ] ModelCache auto-snapshots models, hydrates on repeat load
- [ ] diffusers/DiffusionPipeline support
- [ ] ComfyUI and vLLM integrations
- [ ] CLI: --accelerate flag, warm-model command, cache management
- [ ] Qwen2.5-7B loads in ~11s vs ~42s (measured on RTX 4090)

## Architecture
- Layer 1: Transparent monkey-patch hooks (from_pretrained, safetensors, torch.load)
- Layer 2: ModelCache with auto-snapshot, mmap hydrate, LRU eviction
- Layer 3: Framework integrations (ComfyUI, vLLM, generic serving)
- Builds on existing snapshot.py (proven: 3.8x speedup measured)

## Plan Reference
- planning/accelerate/README.md
- planning/accelerate/01-accelerate-hooks.md
- planning/accelerate/02-model-cache.md
- planning/accelerate/03-integrations.md

title: Transparent Hooks (accelerate.py)
description:
Single entry point that monkey-patches framework loading functions for transparent acceleration. No user code changes required.

## Scope
- accelerate() / decelerate() API with hook registry
- Hook 1: transformers PreTrainedModel.from_pretrained — force low_cpu_mem_usage, cache integration
- Hook 3: safetensors.torch.load_file — network volume detection, eager read fallback
- Hook 4: torch.load — .bin to safetensors conversion on first load
- CLI --accelerate flag wired into run.py

## Plan Reference
- planning/accelerate/01-accelerate-hooks.md

title: Implement hook registry and accelerate()/decelerate() API
description:
Create accelerate.py with the hook registry pattern and main entry points.

## Files
- python/zerostart/accelerate.py (new)
- python/zerostart/__init__.py (export accelerate/decelerate)

## Approach
- _hooks list of (name, patch_fn, unpatch_fn) tuples
- accelerate() tries each hook, only installs if target module importable
- decelerate() reverses all hooks
- Config object passed to hooks (cache_dir, auto_cache, network_volume_fix)

## Plan Reference
- planning/accelerate/01-accelerate-hooks.md (Hook Registry)

title: Implement transformers from_pretrained hook
description:
Monkey-patch PreTrainedModel.from_pretrained for transparent acceleration.

## Files
- python/zerostart/accelerate.py

## Approach
- Force low_cpu_mem_usage=True (meta device init, skips 75% of load time)
- Check ModelCache before calling original (cache hit → hydrate)
- Auto-snapshot after first load (cache miss → snapshot for next time)
- Skip cache for quantized models (hardware-specific)
- Include device_map, dtype, revision in cache key

## Plan Reference
- planning/accelerate/01-accelerate-hooks.md (Hook 1)

title: Implement safetensors network volume fix hook
description:
Patch safetensors.torch.load_file to use eager read on network volumes.

## Files
- python/zerostart/accelerate.py

## Approach
- _is_network_volume() checks /proc/mounts for FUSE/NFS/overlay filesystem types
- If network volume: read entire file into memory, then safetensors.torch.load(data)
- If local: use original load_file (mmap is fast on NVMe)
- This fixes 30-50x slowdown on RunPod/Vast persistent volumes

## Plan Reference
- planning/accelerate/01-accelerate-hooks.md (Hook 3)

title: Implement torch.load → safetensors conversion hook
description:
Patch torch.load to convert .bin checkpoints to safetensors on first load.

## Files
- python/zerostart/accelerate.py

## Approach
- Only intercept .bin files (str/Path ending in .bin)
- Check if safetensors conversion exists in cache
- If yes: load via safetensors.torch.load_file (fast mmap)
- If no: load normally, convert result dict to safetensors, cache
- Uses ModelCache.save_as_safetensors / safetensors_path_for

## Plan Reference
- planning/accelerate/01-accelerate-hooks.md (Hook 4)

title: Validate: Transparent Hooks
description:
Validate the accelerate hooks work correctly.

## Test Commands
- python -c "import zerostart; zerostart.accelerate(); zerostart.decelerate()"
- GPU test: accelerated from_pretrained on gpt2 (small model, fast)
- Verify second load is faster than first (cache hit)
- Verify decelerate() restores original functions

## Testing Approach
- Unit test: hook install/uninstall without GPU
- GPU integration test: from_pretrained speedup measurement
- Edge case: accelerate() when transformers not installed (should be no-op)

## Plan Reference
- planning/accelerate/01-accelerate-hooks.md (Testing)

title: Model Cache (model_cache.py)
description:
Manages cached model snapshots on disk with auto-snapshot, mmap hydrate, and LRU eviction.

## Scope
- ModelCache class with has/save/load/evict/list_entries/auto_evict
- Cache key derivation (model_id + dtype + revision)
- Index tracking (sizes, last access, metadata)
- .bin → safetensors conversion cache
- Auto-detect cache location (volume, env var, default)
- Wraps existing snapshot()/hydrate() from snapshot.py

## Plan Reference
- planning/accelerate/02-model-cache.md

title: Implement ModelCache class with save/load/has
description:
Core ModelCache class wrapping snapshot.py with cache management.

## Files
- python/zerostart/model_cache.py (new)

## Approach
- Auto-detect cache dir: ZEROSTART_MODEL_CACHE env, /volume/zs-models, ~/.cache/zerostart/models
- cache_key() derivation from model_id + dtype + revision
- save() normalizes input (nn.Module, dict, DiffusionPipeline) then calls snapshot()
- load() calls hydrate() then touches access time
- has() checks manifest.json exists
- Index file (index.json) tracks metadata per entry

## Plan Reference
- planning/accelerate/02-model-cache.md

title: Implement cache eviction and management
description:
LRU eviction and listing for ModelCache.

## Files
- python/zerostart/model_cache.py

## Approach
- list_entries() returns CacheEntry dataclass (key, model_id, size_bytes, created, last_accessed)
- evict() removes cache dir + updates index
- auto_evict(max_size_bytes) sorts by last_accessed, evicts oldest until under limit
- safetensors_path_for() and save_as_safetensors() for .bin conversion cache

## Plan Reference
- planning/accelerate/02-model-cache.md (Eviction)

title: CRIU Process Snapshots (Tier 1)
description:
Add Tier 1 process snapshots to zerostart — restore a fully initialized Python service in ~5s using CRIU.

## Architecture
- zs-snapshot Rust crate wraps CRIU dump/restore + metadata
- Python SDK exposes ready() + on_restore hooks
- Supervisor in run.py orchestrates cold-start vs restore state machine
- Environment fingerprint validates snapshot freshness

## Success Criteria
- [ ] zs-snapshot crate: doctor, dump --leave-running, restore --restore-detached
- [ ] Python SDK: ready() touches file, on_restore dispatches via SIGUSR1
- [ ] Supervisor: cold-start → dump on first run, restore on subsequent runs
- [ ] Env fingerprint prevents stale restores
- [ ] Linux CRIU integration test passes

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/criu-process-snapshots/README.md
- ../private-zerostart/planning/criu-process-snapshots/01-architecture.md
close_reason: Deferred — CRIU Tier 1 not viable on container GPU providers. VM boot time (~3-5min) negates snapshot restore benefit (~2s). Focus on Tier 2/3 (zs-fast-wheel + progressive loading) which works on containers.

title: Python SDK: ready() + on_restore hooks
description:
Implement minimal Tier 1 SDK in python/zerostart/sdk.py.

## Scope
- ready(): no-op when unmanaged, touches ZEROSTART_READY_PATH when managed
- on_restore decorator: registers callbacks, dispatches via SIGUSR1 on worker thread
- Update __init__.py exports

## Key Design
- Signal handler stays tiny — only sets threading.Event
- Callbacks run on background worker thread, not in signal handler
- Callbacks must be idempotent, failures logged without stopping later callbacks

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/criu-process-snapshots/04-python-sdk.md
close_reason: All SDK tasks complete. ready() + on_restore implemented and validated with 10 tests.

title: Implement ready() in sdk.py
description:
Create python/zerostart/sdk.py with ready() function.

## Files
- python/zerostart/sdk.py (new)

## Approach
- If ZEROSTART_MANAGED not set: no-op return
- Otherwise: touch Path(ZEROSTART_READY_PATH), creating parents
- Keep it minimal — no logging, no exceptions on missing env var

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/criu-process-snapshots/04-python-sdk.md (ready() section)
close_reason: Implemented ready() in sdk.py — no-op when unmanaged, touches ZEROSTART_READY_PATH when managed

title: Implement on_restore decorator with SIGUSR1 dispatch
description:
Add on_restore to python/zerostart/sdk.py with signal-based callback dispatch.

## Files
- python/zerostart/sdk.py (extend)

## Approach
- _on_restore_callbacks list, _restore_event threading.Event
- on_restore(fn) decorator: registers callback, ensures restore thread started
- _restore_signal_handler: tiny — only sets _restore_event
- _restore_loop: background daemon thread, waits on event, runs callbacks with exception logging
- _ensure_restore_thread: lazily starts background thread + registers SIGUSR1 handler (only in managed mode)
- Callbacks must be idempotent, failures logged without stopping later callbacks

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/criu-process-snapshots/04-python-sdk.md (on_restore section)
close_reason: Implemented on_restore decorator with SIGUSR1 dispatch on background thread

title: zs-snapshot Rust crate: CRIU wrapper
description:
New crate at crates/zs-snapshot wrapping CRIU dump/restore with metadata management.

## Scope
- SnapshotConfig, SnapshotId, SnapshotMetadata, RestoredProcess types
- load_metadata / write_metadata helpers
- dump_leave_running() — shells out to criu dump --leave-running
- restore_detached() — shells out to criu restore --restore-detached
- doctor() — checks Linux, criu binary, version, permissions
- SnapshotError enum with thiserror
- CLI binary: zs-snapshot doctor|dump|restore

## Filesystem Layout
~/.cache/zerostart/snapshots/<intent_hash>/images/ + metadata.json + logs
/tmp/zerostart/<run_id>/restored.pid (caller-provided)

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/criu-process-snapshots/02-zs-snapshot-crate.md
close_reason: All tasks complete, crate validated

title: Scaffold zs-snapshot crate with types and errors
description:
Create new crate at crates/zs-snapshot with core types.

## Files
- crates/zs-snapshot/Cargo.toml (new)
- crates/zs-snapshot/src/lib.rs (new)
- crates/zs-snapshot/src/types.rs (new)
- crates/zs-snapshot/src/error.rs (new)
- crates/Cargo.toml (add workspace member)

## Approach
- SnapshotConfig { cache_dir, dump_timeout, restore_timeout }
- SnapshotId { intent_hash }
- SnapshotMetadata { intent_hash, env_fingerprint, entrypoint, argv, python_version, platform, created_at, image_size_bytes, dump_duration_ms, restore_count, last_restored_at }
- RestoredProcess { pid, restore_duration_ms }
- SnapshotError enum with thiserror: CriuNotFound, InsufficientPermissions, NotFound, Corrupted, DumpFailed, RestoreFailed, DumpTimeout, RestoreTimeout, Io, Serde
- Edition 2024, use chrono for timestamps, serde for serialization
- No unwrap/expect — use ? and thiserror

title: Implement dump_leave_running() CRIU dump
description:
Implement async CRIU dump with --leave-running.

## Files
- crates/zs-snapshot/src/dump.rs (new)

## Approach
- dump_leave_running(config, id, pid, metadata) -> Result<SnapshotMetadata>
- Create images dir
- Shell out to: criu dump --tree <pid> --images-dir <images> --leave-running --shell-job --tcp-established --ext-unix-sk --file-locks --log-file dump.log -v4
- Timeout via tokio::time::timeout(config.dump_timeout)
- On success: write metadata with image_size_bytes + dump_duration_ms
- On failure: capture stderr, read dump.log, return DumpFailed with details
- Copy dump.log to snapshot dir

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/criu-process-snapshots/02-zs-snapshot-crate.md (CRIU dump section)
close_reason: dump_leave_running shells out to criu dump with timeout

title: Implement restore_detached() CRIU restore
description:
Implement async CRIU restore with --restore-detached.

## Files
- crates/zs-snapshot/src/restore.rs (new)

## Approach
- restore_detached(config, id, pidfile) -> Result<RestoredProcess>
- Shell out to: criu restore --images-dir <images> --shell-job --tcp-established --ext-unix-sk --file-locks --restore-detached --pidfile <pidfile> --log-file restore.log -v4
- Timeout via tokio::time::timeout(config.restore_timeout)
- On success: read pid from pidfile, compute restore_duration_ms, update metadata restore_count + last_restored_at
- On failure: capture stderr, read restore.log, return RestoreFailed
- Copy restore.log to snapshot dir

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/criu-process-snapshots/02-zs-snapshot-crate.md (CRIU restore section)
close_reason: restore_detached shells out to criu restore with timeout

title: Add zs-snapshot CLI binary
description:
Add CLI so Python runner can invoke zs-snapshot via subprocess.

## Files
- crates/zs-snapshot/src/main.rs (new)

## Approach
- clap derive with 3 subcommands: doctor, dump, restore
- doctor: run doctor(), print report as JSON
- dump --intent-hash <hash> --pid <pid> --metadata <file>: read metadata from file, call dump_leave_running
- restore --intent-hash <hash> --pidfile <file>: call restore_detached, print restored pid
- Use anyhow for CLI error handling
- Exit codes: 0=success, 1=error
- Print structured JSON output for machine parsing by Python

title: Ready detection + Supervisor orchestration

Wire ready detection and snapshot supervisor logic into python/zerostart/run.py.

Compute snapshot intent hash from user inputs.

## Approach
- compute_intent_hash(entrypoint, argv, python_version, platform) -> str
- Hash entrypoint path + entrypoint content hash + argv + python version + platform + mode(process)
- Use hashlib.sha256, return hex digest (truncated to reasonable length)
- Deterministic: same inputs always produce same hash

title: Implement supervisor state machine in run.py
description:
Wire the cold-start vs restore state machine into python/zerostart/run.py.

## Files
- python/zerostart/run.py (modify)
- python/zerostart/snapshot.py (extend)

## Approach
- On startup: compute intent_hash, compute env_fingerprint, load metadata via zs-snapshot CLI
- Restore path: if metadata valid + env_fingerprint matches → zs-snapshot restore → read pid → SIGUSR1
- Cold start path: launch child with ZEROSTART_MANAGED=1 + run dir env vars → wait_for_ready → zs-snapshot dump
- Dump failure is non-fatal (warn and keep serving)
- Signal forwarding: SIGINT/SIGTERM forwarded to child/restored pid
- Supervisor stays alive, polls /proc/<pid> for liveness

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/criu-process-snapshots/01-architecture.md (State Machine)
- ../private-zerostart/planning/criu-process-snapshots/03-ready-integration.md (Snapshot Trigger)
close_reason: Implemented supervisor state machine with cold-start vs restore paths, signal forwarding, dump/restore via zs-snapshot CLI. 6 tests pass.

title: Fast-Wheel Progressive Loading
description:
Replace single atomic uv pip install with a streaming, per-package pipeline that makes each package importable as soon as its wheel is safely committed. The app starts running at the same time as the first download.

Phase 1 is a hybrid installer: uv resolves and handles tiny/metadata-sensitive wheels, fast-wheel daemon handles medium/large wheels with progressive extraction and demand-driven priority.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] fast-wheel daemon extracts each wheel atomically into site-packages
- [ ] Lazy import hook blocks until wheel is committed (done/ marker)
- [ ] Demand file reprioritizes downloads based on actual import order
- [ ] Hybrid orchestrator coordinates uv + fast-wheel daemon
- [ ] Cold start benchmark shows material speedup over baseline uv install
- [ ] Warm path reuses cached artifact plans and completed environments

## Architecture
- Rust daemon reads JSON manifest, downloads/extracts progressively
- Atomic extraction: staging dir + rename, done/failed markers
- Demand watcher: file-based IPC, reserved slot for just-demanded packages
- Python orchestrator: uv resolve → classify → spawn daemon + hook → run script

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/README.md
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/01-architecture.md
close_reason: Epic complete. Fast-wheel progressive loading fully implemented and validated on GPU.

title: Rust Atomic Extract + Status Markers
description:
Add atomic per-wheel extraction to fast-wheel. Each wheel extracts to a staging directory inside site-packages, then commits into place via rename. Status markers (done/, failed/) signal readiness to the import hook.

## Scope
- extract_wheel_atomic() — staging dir + rename commit
- stream_extract_wheel_atomic() — same for streaming path
- Status directory creation and done/failed marker writing
- Stale staging dir cleanup on startup

## Pre-conditions
None — this is foundational work on existing extract.rs and streaming.rs

## Plan Reference

title: Implement extract_wheel_atomic() with staging dir

Add atomic extraction to extract.rs. Extract wheel contents into a staging directory (.installing-{pkg}-{random}/) inside site-packages, then rename each top-level entry into place.

## Files
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/extract.rs (modify)

## Approach
1. Create .installing-{pkg_name}-{random}/ staging dir inside site-packages
2. Extract wheel contents into staging dir (reuse existing extract logic)
3. Validate staged contents before exposure
4. Move each top-level entry from staging to site-packages via fs::rename
5. Refuse to overwrite existing paths (dedicated env assumption)
6. Remove empty staging dir after commit

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/02-fast-wheel-daemon.md (extract.rs section)
close_reason: extract_wheel_atomic() implemented with staging dir + rename commit. 2 tests passing locally + GPU pod.

4. Same overwrite-refusal and cleanup as extract_wheel_atomic()

title: Add status directory with done/failed markers
description:
Create status directory infrastructure for per-package completion signaling. The import hook uses done/ and failed/ markers as authoritative readiness/failure signals.

## Files
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/pipeline.rs (modify — add status dir creation and marker writing)

## Approach
1. Create status_dir/installing sentinel at startup
2. Create status_dir/done/ and status_dir/failed/ directories
3. After successful wheel commit: touch status_dir/done/{distribution}
4. On failure: write error message to status_dir/failed/{distribution}
5. Remove status_dir/installing when all wheels are done
6. Clean up stale .installing-* staging dirs from interrupted runs on startup

title: Implement InstallQueue with demand-driven prioritization
description:
Build the priority queue that orders wheel installation. Default: small first, large last. Supports demand-driven reprioritization via prioritize().

## Files
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/queue.rs (new)
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/main.rs (add mod queue)

## Approach
1. InstallQueue with pending (VecDeque), in_progress (HashSet), done (HashSet)
2. new(wheels) sorts by size ascending
3. prioritize(distribution) moves to front of pending (skip if done/in_progress)
4. next() pops from front, moves to in_progress
5. mark_done(distribution) moves from in_progress to done
6. is_empty() for termination check

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/02-fast-wheel-daemon.md (Priority Queue section)
close_reason: InstallQueue with size-sorted priority, demand-driven prioritize(), mark_done/failed. 7 tests passing.

title: Rust Daemon Mode + Demand Watcher
description:
Add daemon subcommand to fast-wheel that runs as a long-lived process, progressively downloading and extracting wheels. Includes demand file watcher for import-driven reprioritization and reserved slot scheduling.

## Scope
- daemon subcommand in main.rs (clap)
- daemon_run() orchestration in pipeline.rs
- DemandWatcher: polls demand file for new distribution IDs
- Reserved demand slot: one download slot kept available for just-demanded packages
- Lifecycle: read manifest → create status dirs → process wheels → remove installing → exit

## Pre-conditions
- Atomic extract (staging + commit) must be working
- Manifest parsing + priority queue must be working

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/02-fast-wheel-daemon.md (daemon_run, demand.rs, Lifecycle sections)
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/01-architecture.md (Demand-Driven Priority, Concurrency Model)

title: Implement daemon_run() orchestration loop
description:
Implement the daemon's main orchestration loop in pipeline.rs. Reads manifest, processes wheels progressively with download + atomic extract + status markers.

## Files
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/pipeline.rs (modify)

## Approach
1. Parse manifest → create status dirs → clean stale staging dirs
2. Create InstallQueue from manifest wheels
3. Main loop: pop from queue → download → atomic extract → mark done
4. Download strategy by size: <1MB in-memory, 1-50MB temp file, >50MB streaming
5. Bounded concurrency via semaphore (existing pattern)
6. Reserve one slot for demand-responsive work
7. Remove installing sentinel when all done, exit

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/02-fast-wheel-daemon.md (daemon_run, Download Strategy Per Size)
close_reason: daemon_run() orchestrates manifest→queue→download→atomic extract→status markers. Tested with real PyPI wheels on GPU pod.

title: Implement DemandWatcher with reserved slot scheduling
description:
Add demand file watcher that reads priority signals from the import hook and reprioritizes the install queue. Keep one download slot reserved for demand-responsive work.

## Files
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/demand.rs (new)
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/pipeline.rs (integrate demand watcher into daemon_run)
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/main.rs (add mod demand)

## Approach
1. DemandWatcher: reads status_dir/demand file, tracks seen_lines
2. poll() returns newly demanded distribution IDs
3. Poll on 100ms interval (tokio::time::interval)
4. On new demand: call queue.prioritize(distribution)
5. Reserved slot: keep one semaphore permit unclaimed until demand arrives or queue nearly drained
6. Integration: spawn demand watcher as tokio task alongside download workers

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/02-fast-wheel-daemon.md (demand.rs, Priority Queue policy)
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/01-architecture.md (Demand-Driven Priority)
close_reason: DemandWatcher polls demand file for new distributions, prioritizes queue. 4 unit tests + integrated into daemon loop.

title: Implement ArtifactPlan resolution via uv pip compile
description:
Build the resolution layer that produces an exact artifact plan from requirements. Uses uv pip compile for resolution, then resolves exact wheel URLs with hash/index fidelity.

## Files
- python/zerostart/resolver.py (new)

## Approach
1. resolve_requirements(requirements, python_version, platform) → ArtifactPlan
2. Shell out to uv pip compile with --no-header --annotation-style=line
3. Parse resolved output into ArtifactPlan dataclass
4. ArtifactPlan contains: list of WheelArtifact (url, hash, distribution, import_roots, size)
5. Classify wheels: tiny (<1MB, uv handles) vs medium/large (fast-wheel handles)
6. Generate import_name → distribution mapping for the hook
7. Cache plan by hash(requirements + python_version + platform)

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/03-orchestrator.md (Resolution section)
close_reason: Implemented resolver.py, cache.py, test_resolver.py - all 11 tests passing

title: Implement environment cache with plan-keyed reuse
description:
Build environment caching so the warm path reuses completed environments instead of reinstalling.

## Files
- python/zerostart/cache.py (new)

## Approach
1. env_key = sha256(json(plan.cache_key_payload, sort_keys=True))
2. Layout: .zerostart/plans/{env_key}.json, .zerostart/envs/{env_key}/, .zerostart/envs/{env_key}.tmp/
3. If envs/{env_key}/ exists and marked complete → reuse immediately
4. If only plan exists → resume/complete the install
5. Build new envs in .tmp/ then finalize via rename
6. ensure_plan_environment(plan) → returns env path and is_complete flag

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/03-orchestrator.md (Environment and Cache Model)

title: Update run.py with hybrid uv + fast-wheel daemon flow
description:
Rewrite the orchestrator to coordinate uv (tiny wheels) + fast-wheel daemon (medium/large wheels) in parallel, with lazy import hook.

## Files
- python/zerostart/run.py (modify)

## Approach
1. resolve → classify → check env cache (warm path: exec immediately)
2. Start uv thread for tiny wheels
3. Generate manifest, spawn zs-fast-wheel daemon subprocess
4. Install lazy import hook with import_name→distribution map
5. Run user script (exec compile)
6. Cleanup: remove hook, join uv thread, wait for daemon, remove status dir

title: PyO3 bindings for fast-wheel daemon
description:
Replace subprocess + filesystem IPC with direct PyO3 bindings. Python calls Rust daemon directly, signals demand via function call instead of file polling, and gets completion notifications instantly.

## Scope
- PyO3 module: zs_fast_wheel (Python-importable Rust extension)
- DaemonHandle: start daemon, signal demand, check/wait done
- Replace 100ms file polling with direct Rust→Python notification
- maturin build setup

## Why
- Eliminates subprocess spawn overhead
- Instant demand signaling (vs 100ms file poll)
- No JSON serialization for manifest (Python dict → Rust struct)
- Cleaner API for orchestrator

## Plan Reference
- ../private-zerostart/planning/fast-wheel-progressive/02-fast-wheel-daemon.md
close_reason: PyO3 bindings complete: DaemonHandle with start/signal_demand/wait_done/wait_all/stats/shutdown. Validated on GPU pod.

title: Create PyO3 module with DaemonHandle
description:
Create a PyO3 Python extension module that exposes the daemon as a callable API instead of a subprocess.

## API
- zs_fast_wheel.start_daemon(manifest: dict) → DaemonHandle
- DaemonHandle.signal_demand(distribution: str) — instant priority bump
- DaemonHandle.is_done(distribution: str) → bool
- DaemonHandle.is_failed(distribution: str) → Optional[str]
- DaemonHandle.wait_done(distribution: str, timeout: float) → bool — blocks until done
- DaemonHandle.wait_all(timeout: float) → bool
- DaemonHandle.shutdown() — clean stop

## Files
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/lib.rs (new — PyO3 module)
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/Cargo.toml (add pyo3, lib target)
- pyproject.toml or maturin config

## Approach
- Reuse existing daemon.rs, queue.rs, manifest.rs internals
- DaemonHandle holds Arc<Mutex<InstallQueue>> + tokio runtime handle
- signal_demand() directly calls queue.prioritize() — no file IPC
- wait_done() uses tokio::sync::Notify or watch channel — no polling
close_reason: DaemonEngine with in-memory state + PyO3 DaemonHandle wrapper. 33 tests passing.

title: Add per-package completion notification channel
description:
Replace done/ file polling with a Rust notification channel that Python can wait on.

## Files
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/daemon.rs (modify — add notify on completion)
- crates/zs-fast-wheel/src/lib.rs (expose wait_done via channel)

## Approach
- Add tokio::sync::broadcast or watch channel for completion events
- Each wheel completion sends (distribution, Ok/Err) to channel
- Python wait_done() blocks on channel receiver filtered by distribution
- Keeps file-based done/ markers too (for CLI mode / debugging)
close_reason: Completion notification via tokio watch channels, integrated into DaemonEngine. No file-based IPC needed.

title: Update orchestrator to use PyO3 DaemonHandle
description:
Update run.py to import zs_fast_wheel and use DaemonHandle instead of subprocess.Popen.

## Files
- python/zerostart/run.py (modify)
- python/zerostart/lazy_imports.py (modify — use DaemonHandle for demand)

## Approach
- import zs_fast_wheel
- handle = zs_fast_wheel.start_daemon(manifest_dict)
- Hook calls handle.signal_demand(dist) instead of writing demand file
- Hook calls handle.wait_done(dist, timeout) instead of polling done/ dir
- Fallback: if PyO3 module not available, fall back to subprocess mode
close_reason: Rewrote run.py and lazy_imports.py to use DaemonHandle. No file-based IPC.
